900 lines of code per file... phew 😅 

Assuming this PR only moves things around without changing any functionality (i didn't look in details), I think leaning towards smaller and modular files is a great idea. Thanks for the busy work!

Thanks, @emontnemery 👍

Thanks! Probably the pythonhosted docs can't be updated any more. It's a legacy hosting. We should migrate to ReadTheDocs which is recommended way to host the docs. I think it can be done in separate PR.

I tested it at my customers site who has lots of keywords in lots of different fields. Overall: It works like a charm, is super fast and way better to work with than ever before.

I tested it on Plone 5.2.6 and only found minor possible improvements:
- the delimiter line between keyword selection and button is a bit confusing.
- button text is really long: Rename All Selected Keywords". Same for "Delete...". I think "Rename" or "Delete" would be enough - there is no other possible context.
- I think your PR targets Plone 6. Would it be possible to add some classes, so label positioning in Plone 5.2 works? I think its not much more.
- Click on search (magnifier icon in the list) loads a new site. Idea: Open result in modal? 

Good work! I love it!
LGTM

It looks like your commits have not been signed-off. To add your Signed-off-by to every commit in your branch:

- Ensure you have a local copy of your branch by checking out the pull request locally via command line
- In your local branch, run: `git rebase HEAD~1 --signoff`
- Force push your changes to overwrite the branch: `git push --force-with-lease origin main`

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that c.session.Accept() blocks until c.session.Close() is closed, which happens in c.Close(), but c.Close() also locks c.m, so we have to release it while c.session.Accept() runs, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, this deadlock would occur, but go unnoticed.

<details>
<summary>Emoji Key</summary>


| Emoji | Meaning                                          | Blocking |
|-------|--------------------------------------------------|----------|
| ±     | Requested change                                 | Yes      |
| ❓    | Query (Request for context or explanation)       | Yes      |
| ❔    | Query (Request for context or explanation)       | No       |
| 💭    | Note/Question to myself or the @-mentioned user. | No       |
| ↔     | "Here's another way to do this:"                 | No       |

A couple high level points:
1. I think we should limit the amount we make modifications to the superclass Trainer inside the subclass Trainer, and try to fit in with the superclass APIs as much as possible. HFTrainer will serve as the primary example for how we integrate other Trainers in the future, so it would be great to leverage the APIs that we have built.
2. I also think we should limit the amount we are modifying Huggingface behavior and try to use their public APIs as much as possible, leave validation & error handling to them, and fail quickly if the user tries to do things that we don't support instead of overriding this behavior to get this to work. This will make sure our implementation is easy to maintain and is future-proof (after all they are working on transformers v5 and a lot of their APIs will be changing). This was a mistake I made with ray-lightning, and it's been a pain to maintain it, so hoping not to repeat this again! Good thing is, if their APIs are not flexible enough to support our use cases, we can work with the HF team to make the necessary changes.

I think there's probably some more detail to go into here, but my skim of the extraction AST parsing code looks reasonable to me.

The main blocker is that we have to generate JSON files on a per frontend bundle basis, not per plugin. This is because different Javascript assets may be injected into different SPAs (for example the side nav bundles are used on every SPA and need strings).

To avoid having to load all the strings for every plugin on every page, we should reinstate the per-bundle JSON generation. We can maintain per plugin CSV generation for translator convenience.

So, we can use our plugin parsing machinery that we use for code building, and use this to also generate the file paths we search in for string extraction.

In the process of this, we can also extract the specific webpack configurations for each plugin (which will allow us to properly traverse aliased import paths). We can then also use the webpack configuration to discover the entry points of each of our frontend bundles, and extract the appropriate strings for each of those into the relevant bundle JSON from our translated CSVs.

This means that we still get to upload strings from unimported files, because we are extracting strings via globbing, but we are only loading used strings at runtime. Further, it means that if someone has been a bit naughty and has imported strings from another plugin, then we can allow at JSON generation time for that string to be duplicated into the JSON files for multiple frontend bundles, meaning we won't get caught out by cross component translation issues.

I haven't looked much at the code in particular but  I did notice that there is only one root `.gitignore` file in the eyes of the exclusion code. This is a problem since files outside of the scope (or should I say folder) of a `.gitignore` file would still be subject to its exclude rules.

Let's say we have a directory structure like this: 
```
.
├── a
│   ├── a.py
│   ├── b.py
│   └── .gitignore
├── a.py
├── b.py
├── c.py
└── .gitignore
```

And with `./.gitignore` containing `c.py` and `./a/.gitignore` containing `b.py`. 

I would expect that only `./c.py` and `./a/b.py` would be ignored by Git and Black. This is true with Git, but not with Black. Black also ignores the root level `b.py` file because the nested `.gitignore`'s contents was added to the "root" `.gitignore` that all the files and directories Black finds recursively are subject to.

```console
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/oss/black$ gh pr checkout 1750
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ git status
On branch master
Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	a.py
	a/a.py
	b.py

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
(black) ichard26@acer-ubuntu:~/programming/testing/gitignore$ black .  --check -v
.git ignored: matches the --exclude regular expression
c.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
a/b.py ignored: matches the .gitignore file content
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a/a.py already well formatted, good job.
/home/ichard26/programming/testing/gitignore/a.py already well formatted, good job.
All done! ✨ 🍰 ✨
2 files would be left unchanged.
```

Got a general suggestion on how you could tweak this functionality slightly, so I'll save any small review points for now...

As I understand it this is piggybacking on the existing language version functionality, so using `owner` and differentiating the versions by number and then getting the latest version for a language (if enabled) in the middleware.

However, I think there's an important distinction between the language versions and "content" versions of pages. We only know what language a user wants at the point a request is made, so we have to look for the correct version in the middleware. With content versions we already know which version we should show to the user before the request is made because we don't care about old versions (except for staff members).

There might be another way of handling it but here is my suggestion:
Add another foreign key relation to `page` on the `Page` model and call it something like `version_for`, keep `version` for ordering purposes and `verbose_name` `owner` to something more language specific. Then in the `PageManager` by default we can filter out any pages that have `version_for` set and we can either add another middleware class `VersioningMiddleware` or extend `PublicationMiddleware` to serve up different versions on the frontend for admins.

Caveats to the above:
- It should only be possible to make versions for pages that don't have `version_for` and `owner` set, essentially just the root page. Alternatively: restrict versions to the language of its parent.
- Content will need to be copied over when making a page the 'live' version (or you could shuffle the foreign keys to make it the root object but that seems more sketchy)
- When updating a language version it will have to look at its owner's `version_set`

```python
class ValueDifferenceMetric:

I think I follow the method from reading the code. You basically compute an equation that is the integral of the interpolant over an image. It's linear so you solve it using a direct solver. 

The tests look complete. I cannot comment on the other random bug fixes. 

I do have one performance suggestion. If I understand the method correctly, for a fixed image size and interpolant type, the matrix A that you run through the LLT decomposition is independent of the underlying image. I am betting most of the compute time is this decomposition and not the final solve step (called back substitution IIRC). I am guessing most folks dealing with psf models will draw with the same size and interpolant always (or would be willing to anyways). Thus if we cache the matrix decomposition, we should be able to have a fair more efficiency in the code. I also could be misunderstanding things. 

Final note is that I did not check the unit integrals of the interpolants in detail. However the tests look like they cover any possible mistakes in the analytic ones.

I have got a couple of things that I would like to see addressed that I believe should be pretty easy to do and leave the code in better shape. I don't want this to be a blocker though, so that's why I'm giving my approval. These recommendations are optional, and you can do what you want with them.

First, I think we should organize our `plugins` module so that it has room to grow. This can be accomplished by adding a sub-module `virtual_packages` to organize all the new files you added (e.g. `cuda.py`). After doing this, the directory structure would look like this:

```
./plugins
├── __init__.py
└── virtual_packages
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── archspec.py
    ├── cuda.py
    ├── linux.py
    ├── osx.py
    └── windows.py
```

Second, I think we should have a special `register_virtual_packages` function. Perhaps this could live in `conda/plugins/virtual_packages/__init__.py`? I'd like to see all the `pm.register` calls moved there. Here's what that would look like:

```python
def register_virtual_packages(pm: PluginManager) -> None:
   """
   Please add a nice doc string too explaining why this function exists!
   """
    pm.register(archspec)
    pm.register(cuda)
    pm.register(linux)
    pm.register(osx)
    pm.register(windows)
```

This would later be called and imported in the `get_plugin_manager` function.

Also, I think that you should mention something in the documentation about the following virtual package names being reserved:

- archspec
- cuda
- unix
- linux
- libc (more maybe?)
- win
- osx

If someone were to implement their own version of those, it would error out right? That should definitely be documented. It's okay not to do this now, but please make a ticket to do so in the future.

There has been a lot of discussion and comments on this PR as we worked through it, so I'll try to summarize the key points here.

There is also a section in "Writing R Extensions" dedicated to building vignettes, which could be helpful for reviewing: https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-exts.html#Writing-package-vignettes.

**1. Building `{lightgbm}` now requires installing it**

<details><summary>details (click me)</summary>

The package has to now be installed during `R CMD build` in order to build vignettes.

From "Writing R Extensions":

> R CMD build will automatically create the (PDF or HTML versions of the) vignettes in inst/doc for distribution with the package sources. By including the vignette outputs in the package sources it is not necessary that these can be re-built at install time, i.e., the package author can use private R packages, screen snapshots and LaTeX extensions which are only available on their machine.

That means that code like this:

```shell
sh build-cran-package.sh
R CMD INSTALL lightgbm_*.tar.gz
```

will now end up compiling LightGBM twice.

This makes our CI jobs take a bit longer, but the effect on users should be minimal. Vignettes won't be re-built when anyone runs `install.packages()` to install from CRAN. This PR introduces the option `--no-build-vignettes` to `build-cran-package.sh` and `build_r.R` to allow people building from source to opt out of rebuilding the vignettes.

It also means that all of `{lightgbm}`'s dependencies have to be installed before running `R CMD build`.

</details>

**2. `{lightgbm}` now depends on `{knitr}` and `{rmarkdown}`**

<details><summary>details (click me)</summary>

I'm proposing adding these as `Suggests` dependencies. They're not necessary to install the package so users shouldn't be impacted. You can see from the list of "Reverse Suggests" at https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/knitr/index.html and https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/rmarkdown/index.html that this is a common pattern.

</details>

**3. `build-cran-package.sh` now needs to carefully remove object files from the package tarball**

<details><summary>details (click me)</summary>

See this comment from early in the development on this PR: https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3946#issuecomment-778734967.

The installation of the package during `R CMD build` leaves behind `.o` files, and these cannot be distributed with the package. Adding rules to `.Rbuildignore` did not prevent this issue. Neither did adding a `clean:` target to `src/Makevars.in`.

I believe the issue is that R's own code automatically cleans up `.o` files during this stage, but only if they're in the current working directory at build time.

Notice that the objects listed in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3946#issuecomment-778734967 are only those nested under `src/`... `lightgbm_R.o` and `c_api.o` are remmoved correctly.

`R CMD build` calls an internal function `cleanup_pkg()` here:

https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/b2498c9792278f68aa56bc65fab45b0ff9444c53/src/library/tools/R/build.R#L436

But that function's logic will only clean up `*.o` / `*.so` files one level below the current working directory!

https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/b2498c9792278f68aa56bc65fab45b0ff9444c53/src/library/tools/R/build.R#L465-L468

I'm planning to open a bug report with R, but even if that goes well it'll be a long time (like on the order of years) until all versions of R that LightGBM supports have a fix.

So for now, I'm proposing adding code in `build-cran-package.sh` that untars the package, removes those `.o` files, and re-tars it. That code could be removed in the future if https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/issues/3249 is implemented and the R package uses a unity build instead (as @hcho3 suggested in https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/3188#discussion_r458714279).

</details>

Thanks very much!

Thanks for working on this @enricotagliavini! If you merge the current `main` into this branch, the failing job on CircelCI should fix itself.

Did a usage first pass, I have a few high-level pubsub design questions before I dig deeper.

It looks like we're using a channel abstraction in order to be able to piggyback off of shared publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections, where the publisher and subscriber interfaces expose the channel concept to the application code via the channel type and deal only with a generic `PubMessage` message type that oneofs across the messages for the different channels. This then entails pass-through methods to the subinterfaces for each channel, maintaining channel --> data structure maps internally, and leaking what is essentially a transport detail (the long-polling connection) to the application level by requiring publishers/subscribers to specify the channel type. I'm wondering if there's a better way to structure this such that the application code doesn't have to worry about channels and more of this logic is encoded in the type system so we have compile-time guarantees that things are 👌 .

What if we adopted a pattern similar to gRPC's shared transport channel, generalized from managing a 1-to-1 connection to managing 1-to-n connections, where we have a transport broker concept that encapsulates the publisher <--> subscriber long-polling connections. The subscriber interface for each pubsub instance takes a shared pointer to the broker on construction, and can therefore share the broker across different pubsub instances (e.g. separate subscriber objects for `WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION` and `WAIT_FOR_REF_REMOVED`), where new publisher <--> subscriber connections can be registered by any of the pubsub instances at subscription time.

```
subscribers <--> broker <--transport--> broker <--> publishers
```

I think there could be a few advantages here:

1. We could then expose a subscriber interface per pubsub instance, templated on the concrete message type (no need for a proto oneof message to be exposed to the application code, that could be isolated to the broker), which will give better compile-time checking of the application-level pubsub messages and will obviate the need for those passthrough methods in a top-level subscriber interface that requires a channel type to be specified.
2. I think that you'd still need a proto-level channel type enum, but I think that you could get away with specializing the subscriber with _the enum value itself_. E.g. `Subscriber<typename MessageID, typename Message, rpc::ChannelType channel_type>` could be specialized as `Subscriber<ObjectID, rpc::WaitForObjectEvictionMessage, rpc::WAIT_FOR_OBJECT_EVICTION>`, where the `channel_type` is included when interacting with the broker (e.g. subscribe, unsubscribe). Dispatch of messages received in the long-poll response by the broker will still have to use a channel --> subscriber table, but that can be populated dynamically when the subscriber is constructed: `broker_.RegisterSubscriber(channel_type, this)`.
3. The transport-level optimization of sharing long-polling connections is pushed down as an implementation detail of the transport broker concept, and is a natural place to evolve other transport-level details, like switching to a bidi streaming connection or switching to a centralized message broker.
4. Other transport-level things, like the core worker client pool and the subscriber address/port, can be moved to the broker. Publisher addresses will probably continue to be required by subscriber methods, unless we want the broker to always map worker or node IDs to concrete addresses (probably less efficient in some cases?).
5. The current process of registering a new channel will be replaced by adding a new channel type to the proto enum, adding the channel's proto to the broker's pubsub message oneof, templating a subscriber with a new message type and the channel type, and reusing the existing broker. I don't think there should be much more required than that.

I think that you would have an analogous broker abstraction on the publisher side as well (encapsulating the cached long-polling connection, controlling the message batching), although I haven't thought it through as much since I think it should be simpler than the subscriber-side. My guess is that the publisher would be similarly templated, would similarly register itself with the publish-side broker on construction, and each publisher instance (one per channel type) would have its own subscription index. I think that the publisher's view of the subscription, `Subscriber`, would be internal to the publish-side broker.

The cons with this broker approach, as I see them:

1. Broker concept is exposed to the application level, although what's needed to configure that concept (worker client pool, subscriber address/port, etc.) is already exposed to the application level, so I'm not sure if this is any worse?
2. A shared transport-level broker will obviously have to be thread-safe, although that shouldn't be very difficult.
3. Hidden dragons that I haven't thought of.

Lmk what you think about this approach and whether I'm missing anything! This is also something that could wait until a future PR, e.g. when I port the OBOD object location subscriptions or the GCS node/actor tables to this pubsub I'm sure that I'll have to refactor some stuff anyway.

This PR still has a lot of work to do to make it correct. I've marked up some standout places, but functions where there are multiple types being unioned probably want some sort of type alias rather than restating a lot of types over and over again.

Does this pass mypy, as-is? If so, we need to make a check part of CI with this PR, and I can assist in doing that. If not, that has me a bit concerned. People use claripy in a lot of different and sometimes problematic ways, and I don't want to send people down rabbit holes when they update claripy and get a bunch of type errors that are not actually errors, they're just types that weren't represented in the test suite, which is not as complete as it should be. Running against the angr test suite would definitely help to increase the completeness of the types, but will still need a lot of manual work to determine what commonly unioned types need to be an alias, what types are actually accepted but not represented in the suite, etc. Wrong type annotations are arguably worse than no type annotations, so its important to be correct here.
